description: Swahili (like many Bantu languages) has a phonemic contrast between short and long vowels. The lengthening of vowels is distinctive and can change word meaning (e.g., /papa/ 'be flat' vs /paːpa/ 'the rostrum'). This morphological process is typically derived from historical compensatory lengthening after elision of adjacent consonants.
sources: Ashton, E. O. (1947). _Swahili Grammar: Including intonation_. Longmans, Green and Co.
Maho, J. R. (2009). _NUGL Online: The Online Version of the New Updated Guthrie List, a Referential Classification of the Bantu Languages_.
Polomé, E. C. (1967). _Swahili Language Handbook_. Center for Applied Linguistics.
